title: Order Optimal Regret Bounds for Sharpe Ratio Optimization under Thompson Sampling
summary: In this paper, we study sequential decision-making for maximizing the Sharpe ratio (SR) in a stochastic multi-armed bandit (MAB) setting. Unlike standard bandit formulations that maximize cumulative reward, SR optimization requires balancing expected return and reward variability. As a result, the learning objective depends jointly on the mean and variance of the reward distribution and takes a fractional form. To address this problem, we propose the Sharpe Ratio Thompson Sampling \texttt{SRTS}, a Bayesian algorithm for risk-adjusted exploration. For Gaussian reward models, the algorithm employs a Normal-Gamma conjugate posterior to capture uncertainty in both the mean and the precision of each arm. In contrast to additive mean-variance (MV) formulations, which often require different algorithms across risk regimes, the fractional SR objective yields a single sampling rule that applies uniformly across risk tolerances. On the theoretical side, we develop a regret decomposition tailored to the SR objective and introduce a decoupling approach that separates the contributions of mean and variance uncertainty. This framework allows us to control the interaction between the Gaussian mean samples and the Gamma precision samples arising in the posterior. Using these results, we establish a finite-time distribution-dependent $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ upper bound on the expected regret. We further derive a matching information-theoretic lower bound using a change-of-measure argument, showing that the proposed algorithm is order-optimal. Finally, experiments on synthetic bandit environments illustrate the performance of \texttt{SRTS} and demonstrate improvements over existing risk-aware bandit algorithms across a range of risk-return settings.

Abstract
In this paper, we study sequential decision-making for maximizing the Sharpe ratio (SR) in a stochastic multi-armed bandit
(MAB) setting. Unlike standard bandit formulations that maximize cumulative reward, SR optimization requires balancing expected
return and reward variability. As a result, the learning objective depends jointly on the mean and variance of the reward distribution
and takes a fractional form. To address this problem, we propose the Sharpe Ratio Thompson SamplingSRTS, a Bayesian algorithm
for risk-adjusted exploration. For Gaussian reward models, the algorithm employs a Normal-Gamma conjugate posterior to capture
uncertainty in both the mean and the precision of each arm. In contrast to additive mean-variance (MV) formulations, which often
require different algorithms across risk regimes, the fractional SR objective yields a single sampling rule that applies uniformly
across risk tolerances. On the theoretical side, we develop a regret decomposition tailored to the SR objective and introduce a
decoupling approach that separates the contributions of mean and variance uncertainty. This framework allows us to control the
interaction between the Gaussian mean samples and the Gamma precision samples arising in the posterior. Using these results,
we establish a finite-time distribution-dependent O(log n) upper bound on the expected regret. We further derive a matching
information-theoretic lower bound using a change-of-measure argument, showing that the proposed algorithm is order-optimal.
Finally, experiments on synthetic bandit environments illustrate the performance of SRTS and demonstrate improvements over
existing risk-aware bandit algorithms across a range of risk-return settings.
Index Terms
Multi-armed bandit, Thompson sampling, Sharpe ratio, Risk-averse bandits, Concentration inequalities, Online learning.
I. I NTRODUCTION
The Multi-armed bandit (MAB) problem [1] provides a fundamental framework for sequential decision-making under
uncertainty. In the classical stochastic MAB formulation, an agent repeatedly selects from a set of K arms, each associated
with an unknown reward distribution, and observes a stochastic reward. The objective is to maximize the cumulative reward
by balancing exploration and exploitation. A large body of work has characterized this problem, establishing logarithmic upper
and lower bounds on regret, asymptotically efficient allocation rules, and finite-time algorithms such as upper confidence bound
(UCB) and Thompson sampling (TS) that achieve near-optimal logarithmic regret for expected reward maximization [2]‚Äì[4].
Most existing formulations assume risk neutrality, in which arms are evaluated solely by their expected rewards. However,
in many practical applications, including quantitative finance, autonomous robotics, and clinical trials, decision makers must
also account for the variability of outcomes [4], [5]. For example, in portfolio allocation or algorithmic trading, strategies are
evaluated not only by their expected return but also by the associated risk. Early work on risk-aware bandits incorporated risk
through additive mean-variance (MV) objectives, typically written as œÅ¬µ ‚àí œÉ2, where œÅ is a risk-tolerance parameter [6]‚Äì[8].
Although such objectives are analytically convenient, they exhibit structural limitations in extreme risk regimes. In particular,
as œÅ ‚Üí ‚àûor œÅ ‚Üí 0, one component of the objective dominates the other, effectively eliminating the contribution of either
the mean or the variance. To address this issue, several works propose switching between separate algorithms, for instance,
alternating between mean-based and variance-based TS depending on the value of œÅ [8].
An alternative approach is to use the Sharpe ratio (SR), defined as the ratio between expected return and a measure of
variability in the reward distribution [9]. Because the SR takes a fractional form, it provides a scale-invariant measure of
risk-adjusted performance that simultaneously accounts for reward and variability. This formulation naturally couples the mean
and variance, avoiding the need for algorithmic switching across risk regimes. Recent work has therefore considered the SR
and related risk-adjusted metrics directly as bandit objectives [10]‚Äì[12], revealing new statistical and algorithmic challenges
compared to standard reward-maximization problems. Despite its conceptual appeal, optimizing the SR introduces significant
technical difficulties. From a statistical perspective, the algorithm must estimate both the mean and the variance of each arm.
From a mathematical standpoint, the presence of the variance in the denominator produces a nonlinear objective that is not
sub-Gaussian. Since empirical variance estimates follow Gamma or chi-squared distributions and exhibit heavier tails than
Gaussian statistics, classical concentration arguments used in standard bandit analysis are no longer directly applicable. As a
result, most existing theoretical results for SR optimization rely on frequentist UCB-based methods [10]‚Äì[12], which typically
employ conservative union bounds that increase the exploration cost.

In this work, we study SR maximization from a Bayesian perspective and propose a TS algorithm tailored to this objective,
called Sharpe Ratio Thompson Sampling ‚Äì SRTS. The algorithm maintains posterior distributions over the mean and precision
of each arm and selects actions based on sampled SRs. Using this framework, we develop a regret analysis for the fractional SR
objective. In particular, we introduce a regret decomposition that relates cumulative SR regret to problem-dependent quantities
such as the sub-optimal gap and the divergence between reward distributions. For Gaussian rewards, this analysis yields explicit
distribution-dependent regret bounds that characterize how both mean and variance affect learning efficiency. We complement
the theoretical analysis with experiments on synthetic bandit environments. The results illustrate the behavior of SRTS across
different risk regimes and show that it performs favorably compared with existing risk-aware bandit algorithms.
A. Related Literature
The MAB framework has been widely studied for sequential decision-making under uncertainty, with classical formulations
focusing on maximizing expected cumulative rewards [2], [13]. A substantial body of work has analyzed exploration strate-
gies using information-theoretic tools such as mutual information, entropy, and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence to obtain
distribution-dependent regret bounds [14], [15]. However, these formulations assume risk neutrality and evaluate arms solely
by their expected rewards, ignoring higher-order distributional characteristics such as reward variability. In contrast, optimizing
the SR requires estimating both the mean and the variance of the reward distribution, which introduces additional statistical
and algorithmic challenges.
Although the SR originates from financial portfolio theory, it has also been applied in other machine learning settings. For
example, in fairness-aware learning, the Sharpe predictor proposed in [16] evaluates classifiers by balancing predictive accuracy
and fairness. Similarly, in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), a Sharpe-inspired criterion has been used to
guide prompt selection by balancing expected model improvement and uncertainty [17]. These examples illustrate how the SR
can serve more generally as a metric for balancing performance and variability.
1) Risk-aware bandits: Several works have studied risk-sensitive bandit objectives based on MV trade-offs [6], [7]. In [6],
Sani et al. proposed the MV-UCB algorithm, which uses variance as the measure of risk and achieves regret of order O(‚àön).
This result was later improved by [7], which established logarithmic regret bounds. The MV criterion has also been examined
in the full-information setting by [18], where it was shown that achieving sublinear regret under this risk measure is impossible.
Alternative risk formulations have also been considered. For instance, [19] introduced the RA-UCB algorithm based on the
logarithm of the moment-generating function of the reward distribution and derived high-probability regret guarantees.
In the pure exploration setting, percentile-based risk measures such as Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Conditional Value-at-Risk
(CVaR) have also been studied. VaR and CVaR were originally introduced in [20] and [21], respectively. In the bandit context,
[22] investigated risk-averse MAB problems using the VaR of the cumulative reward as the risk criterion and proposed
algorithms with poly-logarithmic regret bounds. The work of [23] established strong concentration guarantees for VaR without
requiring assumptions on the tail behavior of the reward distribution. CVaR-based bandits have also been studied under bounded
reward assumptions [24], [25]. In [24], the guarantees are derived within a risk-neutral bandit framework under the assumption
that the arm with the highest mean reward is also optimal under the CVaR objective. In a related direction, [26] studied the
problem of identifying the arm with the largest Œ±-quantile of the reward distribution. TS approaches for CVaR bandits were
later developed in [27], [28]. In particular, [27] established asymptotic optimality for CVaR bandits with bounded rewards,
while [28] compared CVaR-based TS with lower confidence bound (LCB)/UCB methods and reported improved empirical
performance. A comprehensive overview of risk-aware bandit formulations is provided in [29].
2) Thompson sampling and Sharpe ratio: Among Bayesian approaches, TS [4] has received considerable attention due to
its strong empirical performance and theoretical guarantees. Regret analyses for TS are often expressed in terms of information
gain about the reward distributions [30], [31]. The algorithm has been extensively studied in both Bayesian and frequentist
settings [32]‚Äì[36]. In the context of risk-aware bandits, [37] proposed a TS algorithm for MV objectives. In contrast, theoretical
results for SR optimization in bandits remain limited. Existing approaches such as UCB-RSSR [11] and UCB-SR [12] provide
logarithmic regret upper bounds using UCB-based methods. Some recent works have applied TS heuristically to SR-based
portfolio strategies [38]‚Äì[40], but these studies do not provide formal regret guarantees.
A key challenge in this setting is that minimizing regret with respect to the SR requires learning both the mean and the
variance of the reward distributions. Consequently, the analysis must account for uncertainty in multiple parameters. In the
related MV setting, this theoretical picture is largely complete: algorithms such as MV-UCB [6], [7] and MVTS [8] achieve
O(log n) regret that matches the known ‚Ñ¶(log n) lower bounds. For the SR objective, however, the corresponding information-
theoretic lower bounds have not been established. In this work, we address this gap by developing both upper and lower
regret bounds for SR maximization using a Bayesian TS framework. In particular, we show that the proposed SRTS algorithm
achieves O(log n) regret and matches the corresponding lower bound up to constant factors.
B. Contributions
Our main theoretical and algorithmic contributions are summarized below.
3
‚Ä¢ Regret decomposition for the SR objective: We develop a regret decomposition tailored to the fractional SR objective.
In classical bandits, regret can be expressed as a weighted sum of the expected number of pulls of suboptimal arms,
where the weights correspond to the mean gaps. For the SR objective, the fractional structure couples estimation errors
in the mean and variance, preventing such a direct decomposition. We introduce a decoupling framework that separates
these contributions and enables the expected regret to be expressed as a weighted sum of expected suboptimal pulls, with
weights that explicitly capture the joint effect of mean and variance estimation errors.
‚Ä¢ Finite-time regret bounds for SRTS: We analyze the proposed SRTS algorithm and establish distribution-dependent
finite-time regret bounds. In particular, we show that SRTS achieves O(log n) expected regret under the SR objective,
despite the non-sub-Gaussian nature of the fractional metric.
‚Ä¢ Information-theoretic lower bounds: We derive a model-specific lower bound for SR optimization using a change-of-
measure argument. The result shows that any consistent policy must incur logarithmic regret, matching the order of the
upper bound achieved by SRTS up to constant factors.
‚Ä¢ Empirical evaluation: We conduct experiments on synthetic bandit environments to evaluate the proposed algorithm
across different risk regimes. The results illustrate the behavior of SRTS under varying levels of risk tolerance and show
improved performance compared with existing risk-aware bandit methods.

A key difference between risk-aware bandits and classical risk-neutral formulations lies in the structure of the regret. In

IV. SRTS ALGORITHM FOR THE GAUSSIAN DISTRIBUTION
A primary operational challenge in risk-aware sequential learning is that the optimal exploration-exploitation tradeoff is
highly sensitive to the unknown true moments of the reward distributions and the risk tolerance parameter œÅ. Designing
deterministic confidence bounds that are uniformly tight across all risk profiles is mathematically restrictive. We bypass this
limitation by proposing the SRTS algorithm, a Bayesian policy that intrinsically balances risk and reward by operating directly
on the posterior parameter distributions.

IX. N UMERICAL RESULTS
This section presents numerical experiments designed to illustrate the empirical performance of the proposed SRTS algorithm
and compare it with existing approaches for risk-aware bandit optimization.
A. Experimental Setup and Baselines
We consider a Gaussian bandit environment with K = 10 arms. The true mean and variance parameters are
¬µ = (0.10, 0.27, 0.34, 0.41, 0.43, 0.55, 0.56, 0.67, 0.71, 0.79) and
œÉ2 = (0.05, 0.09, 0.19, 0.14, 0.44, 0.24, 0.36, 0.56, 0.49, 0.85),
which are the same as the experiment from [6]. Unless otherwise specified, the time horizon is n = 20,000. Reported regret
values are averaged over 500 independent Monte Carlo runs. The empirical variability across runs is small relative to the mean
and is therefore omitted from the plots for clarity.
We compare SRTS with two algorithms designed for fractional or risk-aware bandit objectives:
1) U-UCB [43]: an optimistic algorithm for Empirical Distribution Performance Measures (EDPMs) that incorporates a
variance regularization term.
2) UCB-RSSR [11]: a frequentist algorithm for optimizing a regularized squared SR using path-dependent concentration
bounds derived from McDiarmid‚Äôs and Hoeffding‚Äôs inequalities.
B. Behavior Across Risk Regimes
We first examine the behavior of SRTS under different values of the risk parameter œÅ. Figure 2a reports the cumulative
regret on a logarithmic scale for three representative regimes.
14
‚Ä¢ Balanced risk-return ( œÅ = 1 ): In this regime both mean and variance influence the SR objective. The regret grows
approximately logarithmically with time, consistent with the theoretical guarantees.
‚Ä¢ Reward-maximization regime (œÅ = 0): Setting œÅ = 0 removes the variance penalty and reduces the objective to expected
reward maximization. In this case, SRTS behaves similarly to standard TS for stochastic bandits.
‚Ä¢ Variance-dominated regime: To isolate the role of variance, we consider an environment where all arms share the same
mean ¬µi = 1, while retaining their original variances. In this setting, the objective favors arms with smaller variance, and
the algorithm concentrates on the lowest-variance arm over time.
C. Comparison With Existing Algorithms
We next compare SRTS with the baseline algorithms. Figure 2b shows cumulative regret (log scale) as a function of time in
the balanced setting œÅ = 1. In these experiments, SRTS achieves lower regret than both UCB-RSSR and U-UCB. One possible
explanation is that the Bayesian posterior sampling mechanism adapts to the joint uncertainty in the mean and variance
parameters, while frequentist algorithms rely on conservative concentration bounds for the fractional objective.
Finally, Figure 2c examines performance as the risk parameter varies over œÅ ‚àà [10‚àí3, 103]. Across all algorithms, the
cumulative regret decreases as œÅ increases, because a higher œÅ imposes a heavier penalty on variance, thereby widening the
sub-optimality gap between the arms. Across this range, SRTS consistently achieves lower regret than the baseline methods,
indicating stable performance across a wide spectrum of risk sensitivities.
X. C ONCLUSION
This paper studies SR maximization in stochastic multi-armed bandits. Unlike classical reward maximization, the SR objective
is a fractional statistic that depends on both the mean and variance of the reward distribution. This coupling introduces additional
analytical challenges compared with standard bandit objectives. We proposed the SRTS algorithm, a TS policy based on the
Normal-Gamma conjugate posterior for Gaussian rewards. To analyze its performance, we developed a regret decomposition
tailored to the fractional SR objective and introduced a decoupling framework that separates the contributions of the mean
and variance estimation errors. This analysis yields a distribution-dependent O(log n) upper bound on the expected regret. A
complementary change-of-measure argument establishes a corresponding lower bound, showing that the algorithm achieves the
correct logarithmic order.
The proposed framework applies across a broad range of risk sensitivities governed by the parameter œÅ. In particular, the
formulation recovers classical TS behavior as œÅ ‚Üí 0, while maintaining stability in regimes where variance plays a dominant
role. Numerical experiments illustrate the algorithm‚Äôs behavior across different risk regimes and demonstrate competitive
performance relative to existing methods for risk-aware bandit optimization. Overall, the results provide a theoretical and
algorithmic framework for sequential decision-making under SR objectives and illustrate how Bayesian posterior sampling can
be adapted to fractional, risk-sensitive bandit problems.

APPENDIX E
PROOF OF THEOREM 4
This appendix provides the complete mathematical derivation for the model-specific lower bound established in Theorem 4.
To construct this proof, we proceed in three logical steps. First, in Appendix E-A, we derive a pseudo-regret lower bound
(Lemma 2), demonstrating through a covariance decomposition that the cumulative regret is fundamentally bottlenecked by
the expected number of suboptimal arm pulls. Next, in Appendix E-B, we utilize a change-of-measure argument to establish
an information-theoretic lower bound on these pulls (Lemma 3), proving that any uniformly Œ±-consistent policy must sample
suboptimal arms at a rate bounded by their KL divergence. Finally, in Appendix E-C, we synthesize these auxiliary results,
formally evaluating the limits across both asymptotic and finite-time regimes to conclude the proof of Theorem 4.
